text: The video shows the defect on the right side resulting from the abdominoperineal excision performed in the extra levator plane, with the abdominal part of the procedure being conducted laparoscopically.

The surgeon reconstructs the pelvic floor by reflecting the gracilis muscles from their distal insertion into the pelvic defect. The gracilis muscle, visible as the most superficial muscle within the adductor compartment of the thigh, is repositioned to restore pelvic integrity.

Preservation of the neurovascular supply in perineal reconstruction prevents atrophy of the gracilis muscle over time. In the majority of cases, the muscle receives its blood supply from the adductor branch of the profound ephemeris artery, although the contribution of the medial circumflex artery may predominate in some cases. The vascular pedicle is relatively consistent in its location, situated approximately eight to ten centimetres inferior to the pubic tubercle. The muscle is innervated by the anterior branch of the obturator nerve.

The surgeon marks the position of the gracilis muscle with a line from the ischial tuberosity to the medial condyle of the tibia. An initial skin incision is made eight centimeters below the ischial tuberosity and extended through the subcutaneous tissue and deep fascia. The gracilis muscle is mobilized from surrounding loose areolar tissue, separated from adjacent muscles like the adductor longus, using diathermy to cauterize small vessels. Care is taken to avoid the long saphenous vein and medial cutaneous nerve of the thigh.

The surgeon follows the distal tendon of the gracilis muscle subcutaneously to its insertion inferior to the medial condyle of the tibia. The overlying skin and subcutaneous tissue are incised, and the tendon is isolated. The correct tendon is confirmed by pulling on the muscle belly, and the tendon is divided using diathermy, allowing the muscle to be reflected.

The surgeon continues proximal dissection along the body of the gracilis muscle, with small perforating vessels visible entering its deep surface. These vessels are ligated without compromising the muscle's vascular supply. The neurovascular bundle is observed entering the muscle on its deep surface, with the obturator nerve visible entering approximately two centimeters proximal to this point.
The surgeon repeats the procedure on the contralateral side, highlighting the vascular pedicle of the gracilis muscle.

The surgeon uses diathermy to develop a tunnel through the deep fascia between the peroneal defect and the medial compartment of the thigh. Forceps are passed through the tunnel to deliver the gracilis muscle into the peroneal defect, ensuring the muscle is not twisted to maintain its blood supply.

The surgeon closes the skin incision on the thigh in layers, placing a subcutaneous suction drain. The gracilis muscles are woven together and fixed to the distal sacrum and coccyx using permanent sutures. The central tendon is formed by the unipennate configuration of the gracilis muscles, with muscle bellies positioned laterally, and the tendons centrally. The muscles are then attached to the origin of the levator ani.

As the muscle remains innervated, atrophy does not occur and hernia formation is reduced. We believe that this technique represents an elegant method for long-term pelvic floor reconstruction, especially in those patients undergoing laparoscopic abdominal surgery and those post-chemoradiotherapy or with large perineal excisions.
Patients immobilized on day one avoid sitting directly on the wound through the use of specialist cushions. The drains are removed once mobility is independent.
